title: Walls and Gates
description: You are given an m x n grid rooms initialized with these three possible values: -1 representing a wall or an obstacle, 0 representing a gate, and INF (2147483647) representing an empty room. Fill each empty room with the distance to its nearest gate. If it is impossible to reach a gate, it should remain INF.
thinking_questions: You could run BFS from each empty cell to find its nearest gate — O((m·n)²). What if you reversed it?
What if you start BFS FROM all gates simultaneously?
How does multi-source BFS give each cell its distance from the nearest gate in one pass?

hints.L1: Multi-source BFS from all gates (0 cells) simultaneously. Each cell gets the minimum distance automatically.
hints.L2: Add all gates to queue. BFS: for each cell at distance d, set unvisited (INF) neighbors to d+1 and add to queue.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: multi-source BFS from gates — each cell gets nearest gate distance
from collections import deque
INF = 2147483647
q = deque([(r,c) for r in range(rows) for c in range(cols) if rooms[r][c] == 0])
while q:
    r, c = q.popleft()
    for dr, dc in [(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,0)]:
        nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc
        if 0<=nr<rows and 0<=nc<cols and rooms[nr][nc] == INF:
            rooms[nr][nc] = rooms[r][c] + 1
            q.append((nr,nc))
# Time: O(m·n)  Space: O(m·n)

test_cases.expected: each INF cell filled with nearest gate distance

follow_up_questions: Why is multi-source BFS O(m·n) while running BFS from each empty cell is O((m·n)²)?
What if there were multiple types of gates with different weights?
How does this relate to the Rotting Oranges problem structurally?
solution_notes: Multi-source BFS from all gates. Each INF cell gets distance on first visit. O(m·n).
